summary:
The Battle of Diu (February 3, 1509 AD) was one of the most consequential naval engagements in Asian history — a decisive Portuguese victory over a combined fleet of the Mamluk Sultanate of Egypt, the Ottoman Empire, the Zamorin of Calicut, and the Sultan of Gujarat that established Portuguese naval supremacy in the Indian Ocean and shattered the existing Muslim-dominated spice trade network. For centuries, Arab merchants had controlled the lucrative trade routes between Asia and the Mediterranean, carrying spices from the Indies through the Red Sea and Persian Gulf to Alexandria and the Levant. Portugal's [[event:2356|Estado da India]] (State of India) threatened to circumvent this system entirely by sailing directly around Africa; the Muslim trading powers responded by assembling a coalition fleet to destroy the Portuguese presence.

The Portuguese squadron under Francisco de Almeida, though outnumbered, possessed technologically superior cannon-armed warships that could engage effectively from a distance — a decisive advantage over traditional Mediterranean-style galley warfare that relied on ramming and boarding. The Portuguese victory at Diu eliminated the Egyptian fleet as a fighting force and demonstrated that the Indian Ocean's trade could be dominated from the sea by a well-armed European power. This battle opened the Portuguese century of Indian Ocean dominance: the Estado da India established control of key straits and trading ports (Hormuz, Malacca, Goa), extracting tolls from merchants and diverting the spice trade to the Cape of Good Hope route. It marked the beginning of European maritime power's permanent presence in Asian waters.
